key_points: Off-campus options run $400-$1,200/month depending on location
The Landing is ~1 mile from main campus; walkable but not ideal
On-campus Commons dorms (Haymaker) are cramped; lofting beds recommended
key_quotes: it's usually 400-1200 depending on where you're looking. also utilities. the landing has some good options, but is about a mile from campus so it's a decent walk
Pembroke properties on 7th Ave has been good. 650 for a 2br
Since dresser won't fit under the bed, how cramped is it?

key_points: Social scene exists but requires effort; campus can feel dead mid-week
Frats throw parties Thu-Sat; bars accessible but scene is limited
Making friends is hard early on, especially for introverted or LGBTQ+ students
key_quotes: Your best bet is probably clubbing unless you're in frat or a girl.
Thurs–Sat there's usually frat stuff, house parties, or people going out in Huntington
I have been extremely lonely. I have yet to find anyone to hang out with or even talk to for longer than 30 seconds.

key_points: Mandatory meal plan costs ~$1,900 minimum for on-campus students
Very few dining options available; picky eaters will struggle significantly
Flex dollars add complexity; system frustrates students with limited choices
key_quotes: the cheapest meal plan offered was $1900-something, so not cheap at all. This gives me 10 meal swipes and $500 in 'flex' dollars
there are very few [options]
I am honestly kinda a picky eater... I really wish meal plans weren't required

key_points: Loneliness is a real reported issue, especially freshman and transfer students
LGBTQ+ students face added stress around identity in a conservative region
No specific mental health support resources were mentioned or praised
key_quotes: My week has been so filled with panic attacks stemming from this and not being able to eat
My family begged me to not out myself the second I got here, and it has been awful
I'm not trans but I have virtually the exact same experience so far.

key_points: Merit scholarships exist but spring semester funding is notably limited
Out-of-state students should research merit aid carefully before committing
Transfer students report scholarship and funding gaps during semester switches
key_quotes: scholarships and funding are very limited for spring semester
Depends on your HS stats [for merit aid]
I picked a few schools I was interested in and a few of them are very expensive

key_points: Journalism/W Page Pitt School praised by alumni; poli sci also well-regarded
STEM departments are reportedly weak outside cybersecurity and medical fields
Some professors reportedly poor with neurodivergent accommodations despite paperwork
key_quotes: There is a very good chance Marshall will not cure your burnout. It really depends on your career path, but anything STEM, especially non medical, is severely lacking.

As a forensic chemistry graduate, some of the professors in the chemistry and biology department were very difficult to work with. Even with documented paperwork from my doctor saying 'this student ge[ts accommodations]'
I graduated from the W Page Pitt School of Journalism in the early 2000s. I loved it.

key_points: Administration widely described as poorly communicating across departments
Admissions process reported as disorganized and frustrating by multiple students
Bursars and enrollment offices specifically called out as problematic
key_quotes: Marshall is known for having absolutely terrible administrators. This includes Admissions and Bursars. No department on campus communicates to other departments which causes a lot of headaches.
the admission process has been horrible
I fully thought I had selected fall 2026 and not spring 2026... I just sent them an email letting them know about my mistake

key_points: Compact campus praised; not so big you waste time walking between classes
Huntington is safe by WV standards; Ohio River views are genuinely beautiful
Public bus service (TTA) exists but stops are sparse; manual hailing required
key_quotes: At least the university is not so big that one might spend more time going between classes as being IN classes.
Marshall is an extremely safe school in a very safe area.
There is a public bus service called TTA that runs along Hal Greer Blvd... You need to manually hail the bus.

key_points: Alumni report struggling to launch careers without networking or internships
Journalism and poli sci alumni report decent outcomes; law school path exists
One alum with a Marshall degree spent 3 years post-grad working retail
key_quotes: my career has yet to take off 3 years after I graduated. I didn't network nor did I connect with many others, nor did I get an internship.
I have several friends and fraternity brothers who went onto successful careers as lawyers through the Marshall [poli sci] program
Marshall as an educational entity pales in gravity to Marshall University as a business.

key_points: Affordable for in-state students; one of the cheaper WV options
Out-of-state students should compare carefully; Ohio University frequently recommended over Marshall
44-50% graduation rate is a major red flag for tuition investment
key_quotes: Marshall University, a public university with a 90% acceptance rate and 50% graduation rate
If you're thinking about advertising/PR/journalism... you should seriously consider Ohio University over Marshall. Much nicer campus.
Hopefully this doesn't sound like I'm speaking down, but seeing that Marshall University has a 44% graduation rate... I'd say if you work hard, you'd definitely be above your peers

key_points: Strong school spirit around football; 'We Are Marshall' identity is real and felt
Community is generally laid-back and accepting, especially for a WV school
Best for in-state WV students; out-of-staters may find better options elsewhere
key_quotes: About to graduate from Marshall. This place has been the best experience of my entire life so far, I've loved every second of it.
WE ARE........MARSHALL. The University looks amazing!

Having been at both UIUC and Marshall, you would be better served at UIUC, especially in terms of LGBTQ safety and community. If you can't afford UIUC, Marshall is a good option that is relatively nice.

key_points: **Graduation Rate:** Only 44-50% of students graduate — well below national average
**Administration:** Departments don't communicate; admissions and bursars repeatedly cited as dysfunctional
**Academics:** STEM outside medical fields called 'severely lacking' by multiple insiders
key_quotes: Marshall is known for having absolutely terrible administrators... No department on campus communicates to other departments which causes a lot of headaches.
There is a very good chance Marshall will not cure your burnout... anything STEM, especially non medical, is severely lacking.
Marshall University, a public university with a 90% acceptance rate and 50% graduation rate

key_points: HELP program offers real support for neurodivergent and learning-disabled students
Poli sci and journalism departments have dedicated faculty and alumni networks
Compact campus means tight-knit community and easy navigation; no sprawl stress
key_quotes: Contact the HELP program — it's for people with learning disabilities and should help you. When I was at Marshall many [students used it successfully]
I'm a senior poli sci major! The entire department is amazing, especially Dr. Beller.
At least the university is not so big that one might spend more time going between classes as being IN classes.
